eli3: Eigenvectors are special directions that stay on their own line when a matrix transforms space — they might stretch or shrink, but they don't rotate. The eigenvalue tells you how much stretching happens.

Think of a matrix as a stretching machine. Most directions get both stretched and twisted. But eigenvectors are the 'pure' directions where only stretching occurs. Finding them reveals the hidden structure of the transformation.

If v is an eigenvector of A with eigenvalue λ, then Av = λv. The vector doesn't change direction; it just scales by λ. This is incredibly useful for understanding matrices, solving differential equations, and compressing data.

whenWhy.title: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
whenWhy.body: The principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, ordered by eigenvalue magnitude. They give the directions of maximum variance. Eigendecomposition diagonalizes the covariance, decorrelating the data.

whenWhy.title: Stability analysis of dynamical systems
whenWhy.body: For ẋ = Ax, solutions are combinations of e^(λᵢt)vᵢ. If all eigenvalues have negative real part, the system is stable. This applies to control systems, physics simulations, and neural network training dynamics.

whenWhy.title: PageRank and graph analysis
whenWhy.body: PageRank finds the dominant eigenvector (eigenvector with largest eigenvalue) of the web link matrix. This reveals the stationary distribution of a random walk. Spectral clustering uses eigenvectors of graph Laplacians.

whenWhy.title: Matrix powers and exponentials
whenWhy.body: Aⁿ = P Dⁿ P⁻¹ where D is diagonal of eigenvalues. This makes computing matrix powers trivial. Similarly, e^A = P e^D P⁻¹, crucial for solving linear ODEs and continuous-time Markov chains.

deepDiveIntro: For a square matrix A ∈ ℝⁿˣⁿ, a nonzero vector v ∈ ℝⁿ is an eigenvector with eigenvalue λ if Av = λv. This means v spans an invariant subspace: A maps the line through v to itself. The eigenvalues are roots of the characteristic polynomial det(A - λI) = 0.

A matrix is diagonalizable if it has n linearly independent eigenvectors, forming a basis. Then A = PDP⁻¹ where D = diag(λ₁,…,λₙ) and P has eigenvectors as columns. This simplifies computing functions of A: f(A) = P f(D) P⁻¹.

deepDive.heading: Characteristic Equation
deepDive.body: Eigenvalues satisfy det(A - λI) = 0. For an n×n matrix, this is an nth degree polynomial with n roots (counting multiplicity, possibly complex). The sum of eigenvalues equals tr(A); product equals det(A).
deepDive.formula: det(A - λI) = 0, Σλᵢ = tr(A), Πλᵢ = det(A)

deepDive.heading: Eigendecomposition
deepDive.body: If A has n linearly independent eigenvectors, A = PDP⁻¹. D is diagonal of eigenvalues; P has eigenvectors as columns. This diagonalizes A. Not all matrices are diagonalizable (defective matrices lack enough eigenvectors).

deepDive.heading: Symmetric Matrices
deepDive.body: Real symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors. They are always diagonalizable: A = QΛQᵀ where Q is orthogonal (QᵀQ = I). This is the spectral theorem — foundation of PCA and many optimization methods.
deepDive.formula: A = Aᵀ ⟹ λᵢ ∈ ℝ, vᵢ ⟂ vⱼ, A = QΛQᵀ

deepDive.heading: Power Iteration
deepDive.body: To find the dominant eigenvector (largest |λ|), iterate vₖ₊₁ = Avₖ / ||Avₖ||. Converges to eigenvector of largest eigenvalue. With deflation, can find subsequent eigenvectors. Basis of PageRank and many iterative eigenvalue algorithms.

deepDive.heading: Generalized Eigenvalue Problem
deepDive.body: In optimization and physics, we often solve Av = λBv where B is positive definite. This arises in constrained optimization (Lagrange multipliers), vibration analysis, and canonical correlation analysis (CCA).

takeaways: Eigenvectors are directions that only scale under transformation: Av = λv.
Eigendecomposition A = PDP⁻¹ diagonalizes matrices for easier computation.
Symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors (spectral theorem).
Power iteration finds dominant eigenvectors — used in PageRank, PCA.
Sum of eigenvalues = trace; product = determinant.

edgeCases.a: Matrices that don't have enough linearly independent eigenvectors to form a basis. They cannot be diagonalized. Example: Jordan block [[λ, 1], [0, λ]]. These require generalized eigenvectors and Jordan normal form.

edgeCases.q: Can eigenvalues be complex for real matrices?
edgeCases.a: Yes. Complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs for real matrices. They indicate rotation in the plane spanned by the real and imaginary parts of the eigenvector. A 2D rotation matrix has purely imaginary eigenvalues ±i.

edgeCases.q: When is the dominant eigenvalue unique?
edgeCases.a: When |λ₁| > |λ₂| (strictly larger magnitude). Power iteration converges faster when this gap is large. If two eigenvalues have equal magnitude, power iteration may oscillate or converge slowly.

sources.label: Strang — Introduction to Linear Algebra (6th ed.), Ch. 6
